observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:
1.23. We recommended and the Management agreed to:
1.23.1. direct the GSO to complete the annual physical count of the PPEs for the CY
2024 the soonest possible time, and to consider additional personnel to help
in the immediate completion of the annual count and submission of the
RPCPPE to COA;
1.23.2. direct the LGU Officials concerned to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE
as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
1.23.3. direct the GSO to facilitate the immediate disposal of unserviceable and
obsolete properties and furnish the OCACCT with the necessary supporting
documents to enable its derecognition from the books.

Recommendations:
2.19. We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
2.19.1. City Assessor to assist the City Engineer in the conduct of assessment to
establish the fair value of the road lot component of all local roads within the
City Government's jurisdiction;
2.19.2. City Accountant to:
2.19.2.1. recognize road lot valuations as part of the carrying amount in the road
networks account; and
2.19.2.2. maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and RPI, conduct a reconciliation of records
with the GSO to ensure consistency between the list of inventories per
Local Road Inventory Report and Road Map and those recorded in the
RPI and all required disclosures pertaining to the road network systems
in the Notes to Financial Statements.
2.19.3. City General Service Officer to prepare and maintain LRNLC, Local Road
Inventory and Local Road Map.

Recommendations:
3.8. We recommended and the Management agreed to:
3.8.1. direct the CEO to ensure the timely preparation and submission of all required
documentation to the OCACCT upon the completion of projects, (i.e.,
Certificate of Completion, Certificate of Turnover and Acceptance, Status of
Work Accomplished, etc.), to facilitate the proper reclassification of
completed projects into their respective asset accounts and the timely
recognition of depreciation expenses; and
3.8.2. direct the OCACCT to reclassify completed projects under the CIP accounts
to the appropriate asset accounts upon receipt of required documentation and
to subsequently record depreciation expenses.

Recommendations:
4.7. We recommended and the Management agreed to direct:
4.7.1. the Inventory Committee to:
4.7.1.1. conduct a physical inventory; and
4.7.1.2. submit the required Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) in
accordance with the rules and regulations set by COA;
4.7.2. the OCACCT to prepare and maintain a Supplies Ledger Card (SLC);
4.7.3. the CGSO to prepare and maintain a Stock Card (SC); and
4.7.4. the OCACCT and CGSO to reconcile the SLC and SC regularly, and to
implement necessary adjustments or corrections.

Recommendations:
5.5 We recommended and the Management agreed to direct:
5.5.1 the OCACCT to conduct a comprehensive review and analysis of the
circumstances surrounding these long-outstanding balances;
5.5.2 document and compile all efforts undertaken to locate supporting documents
and trace fund utilization;
5.5.3 coordinate with concerned LGUs to obtain any available documents
pertaining to the receipt and utilization of these funds; and
5.5.4 assess accounts for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 by: (i)
evaluating compliance with the prescribed criteria; (ii) filing a request for
write-off authority with the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or
Supervising Auditor (SA), if qualified; (iii) ensuring submission of complete
supporting documents as required under Section 8 of the Circular; and (iv)
adhering to prescribed procedures for verification, analysis and validation of
subject account balances.

Recommendations:
6.17. We recommended Management (or the Local Chief Executive) to direct the
concerned offices to implement the following corrective actions:
6.17.1. the CDRRMO, to prepare and maintain a complete inventory list of all
procured and donated items, as required by Section VI.2 of COA Circular No.
2014-002;
6.17.2. the Property and Supply Unit/CDRRMO, to implement the use of stock cards
for all food and non-food items, in compliance with Section IV.6 of COA
Circular No. 2014-002, to accurately track the receipt, issuance and balance
of all inventory items;
6.17.3. the OCACCT, to establish and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLC) for all
disaster-relief items, as mandated by Section IV.6 of COA Circular No. 2014-
002, reconciling them regularly with the CDRRMO stock cards to ensure
accurate inventory tracking and maintain a necessary check-and-balance
mechanism;
6.17.4. the CDRRMO, to comply with the reporting requirements of Section V.C of
COA Circular No. 2014-002 by regularly and promptly updating the official
city website with reports on DRRMF receipt and utilization; and
6.17.5. the OCACCT, to ensure that all unissued inventory balances of welfare goods
and other supplies are properly classified, valued, and reported in the
Statement of Financial Position, in accordance with the MNGAS for LGUs
and IPSAS 12 on Inventories.

Recommendations:
7.9. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the City Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Office to ensure the timely submission of the
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF within fifteen (15) days after the end
of each month.

Recommendations:
8.10. We recommended and the Management agreed to:
8.10.1. direct the City Accountant to undertake alternative measures, including but
not limited to, sending official letters through barangay officials, announcing
the matter during barangay meetings and posting notices on the LGU’s
official website and other communication channels, to communicate with JO
workers, including those no longer connected with the LGU, for the return of
their unremitted premium contributions; and
8.10.2. require the responsible personnel to promptly reconcile the records of the Due
to Pag-IBIG account, effect the necessary adjustments to correct the records
and remit any outstanding balances to the HDMF, if warranted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the management agreed to:
draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record in the books of accounts the following correcting entry:
Debit:	Receivables – Disallowances/ Charges	790,500.00
Credit:	Prior Period Adjustments			790,500.00
To record COA Disallowances that has become final and executory.
reconcile their recorded disallowances against issued SASDCs to ensure that all issued NFDs are recorded in the books, and all settlements were reflected in the SASDC; and
take appropriate measures and actions to enforce the timely settlement of disallowed transactions as provided in COA Circular NO. 2009-006.

We recommended and management agreed that the:
City Accountant review the schedule of PPE, identify those items that were erroneously capitalized, and make necessary adjusting journal entries to fairly present the PPE account balances; and
offices concerned expedite the process of formally transferring these properties to the end-user agencies in compliance to the above provisions to lessen the property accountability of both CAO and City General Services Office (CGSO).

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant establish the accurate carrying value of all fixed assets by reassessing the estimated useful life and drawing the necessary correcting journal entries.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant revisit the PPE schedues and assess the propriety of each item. The nature of each item should corroborate with its classification and draw necessary adjusting entries to correct the account balances.

We recommended and management agreed to enhance its internal controls on asset management with the procedures and guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, and establish the accurate PPE balances presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that the CAO and CGSO undertake collaborative procedures in earnest, to strengthen their asset management. Both offices should ensure that both their records are regularly updated, significant variances identified, and proper adjustments made.

We recommended and  the management agreed that the:
City Treasurer remit the share of DPWH on building permit fees thru the Bureau of Treasury; and
City’s Building Official prepare a Program of Work (POW) detailing the manner of utilization of the 15% share to be held under trust preferably for the whole year for approval of the LCE, copy furnished the DPWH Secretary thru the National Building Code Development Office (NCBDO).

Henceforth, we recommended that management strictly comply with the prescribed guidelines before granting any special allowance to its personnel. We also recommended that management establish the regularity of the grant to the personnel noted by submitting a detailed accomplishment of each personnel to validate payment thereof.

Henceforth, we recommended and management agreed that the CDRRMC further enhance its procedure in preparing the LDRRMFIP and ensure that identified PPAs and their expected outputs are specific and measurable to aid in assessing program implementation.

We recommended and the management agreed that the LSB revisit the SEF planning and budgeting process to better support the educational goals and priorities of the City and DepEd. We further recommended adherence to the fund utilization requirements stipulated in DepEd-DBM-DILG JMCs No. 1, s. 2017 and Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020.

We recommended and management agreed enhance their monitoring system on fund transfers by having a clear set of terms and conditions for transparency and accountability on fund utilization and project implementation. Moreover, install safeguards from the risk of possible fund misapplication.

We recommended and management agreed that, henceforth, management will improve their process of drafting LDRRMFIP by carefully identifying PPAs to be charged against LDRRMF, ensuring that they comply with Section 21 of RA 10121. We also recommended that the identified PPAs address the four thematic areas of disaster risk management.

Henceforth, we recommended and management agreed:
to expedite the project implementation without compromising the compliance with the Guidebook for Safe Closure and Disposal Sites to ensure that closure will be done in a properly, with established environmental protection measures and safeguards; and
that for future projects relating to environmental protection, fast track the implementation to preclude further damage that will result in budgetary constraints and environmental and health hazards.

We recommended and management agreed to strengthen their screening process by scrutinizing the documents supporting a request for livelihood assistance and conduct an in-depth evaluation of the applicant’s technical and financial capability before granting financial assistance.  We also recommended that the Accreditation Committee enhance its process in evaluating applicants for accreditation to include the procedures stated in Section 4.5.2 of COA Circular 2007-001.

We recommended and management agreed that the MRP: TDD Coordinating Committee amend the existing PPMP and ASPP to align with the amended POW dated February 2, 2021. Henceforth, any changes in the POW should have a corresponding amended PPMP and ASPP.
We also recommended that management make representation to the DOTr to include activities chargeable against Administrative and Miscellaneous Costs that will facilitate the efficient discharge of their responsibilities in the project implementation.

We recommended and the management agreed to direct the OIC-OCEEM device strategies to intensify the marketing and promotional activities of the business center and have all the spaces rented out to maximize income. There is a need to monitor the monthly rent payment of the tenants, including rent delinquencies. Correspondingly, send monthly demand letters to delinquent tenants and post the same to a bulletin board and other conspicuous areas.
Henceforth, conduct an in-depth study before investing in a particular LEE to ensure that the said venture is self-liquidating and serves its purpose of adding revenue in the coffers of government resources.

We recommended and the management agreed to anchor the preparation of GAD AR from its GPB to effectively assess its efficiency in integrating gender awareness and development into its programs and projects. We also recommended that amendments in the GPB include emerging gender issues not considered in the planning phase and submit accordingly to the DILG for review and endorsement.

We recommended and management agreed that GAD Focal Point System revisit the process of preparing the GAD Plan and Budget to aid in management’s evaluation of programs and projects addressing gender issues and concerns. We further recommended the adherence to the requirements of PCW-NEDA-DILG JMC No. 2016-01 concerning the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended and Management agreed that, henceforth, they will enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in compliance with the Rules and Regulations in the Settlement of Accounts as provided for under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended the following courses of actions:

1.	Conduct physical count of all PPEs annually and submit RPCPPE to this Office pursuant to Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs;
2.	For the GSO and CAO to perform periodic reconciliation of property records; and

3.	The Disposal Committee or GSO to conduct inspection and appraisal of the unserviceable properties and recommend for its immediate disposal using the appropriate mode of disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the City Mayor to direct the City’s Gender Focal Point System (GFPS) to set the GAD agenda or identify priority gender issues to be addressed in consultation with local functionaries and stakeholders. The GAD agenda shall be the basis for the annual formulation of PPAs to be included in the city’s GPB. observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to enjoin the following City officials to do the following courses of actions pursuant to COA Circular 2015-008, to wit:
City Accountant
To transfer in the books of accounts the road networks carried in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. If not possible, coordinate with the City Engineer for the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: a) road lot, b) road pavement, c) drainage and slope protection structures, and d) other miscellaneous structures.
Reconcile the subsidiary ledger of the road and its components with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure completeness.
City Engineer
Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads with identified cost;
Determine the components of local roads identified in the inventory without corresponding cost and recognize the cost of each component at its fair value;
Provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
City General Services Officer
In coordination with the City Planning and Development Officer maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map;
Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.
Inventory Committee
Conduct physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network system.

We recommended the following courses of actions:
Conduct physical count of all PPEs annually and submit RPCPPE to this Office pursuant to Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs;
For the GSO and CAO to perform periodic reconciliation of property records; and
The Disposal Committee or GSO to conduct inspection and appraisal of the unserviceable properties and recommend for its immediate disposal using the appropriate mode of disposal.

We recommended to the management the following courses of actions:
City Engineering Office (CEO)
Exhaust all options to identify the details and status of the abovementioned infrastructure projects that were marked as “unidentified”. Furnish the accounting office the details retrieved to facilitate subsequent actions to be undertaken.
Furnish the accounting office the certificates of completion for the completed infrastructure projects that were undertaken by contract. For the completed infrastructure projects undertaken by administration, provide the accounting office the details such as date started, date completed and other important details that would be the basis for the reclassification of CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to its corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
For the ensuing infrastructure projects whether undertaken by contract or administration, provide the accounting office the details through written communication including, among others, the certificates of completion (for projects undertaken by contract), and list/details of infrastructure projects completed and undertaken by administration. With this, the accounting office will have the basis for the reclassification of CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
The City Accounting Office (CAO)
Request from the CEO the copies of certificates of completion for the completed infrastructure projects undertaken by contract.
Request from the CEO the details of the completed infrastructure projects that were marked as “unidentified”.
Upon availability of the documents and other details coming from the CEO, reclassify the completed CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to its corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
For the completed non-infrastructure projects, prepare necessary adjusting entries to take out the accumulated costs of these projects from the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account and charged the same against the City’s government equity.

We recommended the City Accountant to immediately notify the Bank in writing of the reconciling items under General Fund Proper – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-080,  General Fund Proper – LBP Current Account No. 0332-1029-58, OCEEM – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-030, SEF – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-032, and Trust Fund – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-031,  in coordination with the City Treasurer,  to adjust or correct the errors for an accurate and reliable cash account balance every month-end.

We recommended management to:
Direct the City General Services Office  to conduct a physical count of the breeding stocks and coordinate with the Accounting Office to facilitate reconciliation and/or adjustments in the books of accounts; and
Require the focal persons responsible for the breeding stock dispersal of the City Agriculture/Veterinary Office and barangay for the timely submission of relief from property accountability and the required documents on the disposal/death of breeding stocks as required under Sections 151 and 152 of the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Manual in the Local Government for validation and derecognition of the accounts in the books.

We recommended to the City Mayor to direct the City Accountant and all Department Heads to coordinate and adhere to the rules and procedures in accounting for supplies and materials provided under Chapter 7 of the NGAS Manual.
Further, a report on the utilization and disposal of drugs and medicines to end users/beneficiaries be submitted to support the recording of expense account in the books.

We recommended the City Mayor to:
Direct the CAO, CBO and every requesting office to analyze more the transactions and see to it that they are correctly classified as to expense class and recorded in the books of accounts to provide the users of the financial statements, especially the management, correct and adequate financial data of the operations of the City; and
Direct the City Budget Officer and City Accountant to prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries in their respective records to correct the misclassification of the affected accounts. Henceforth, ensure that costs of major repairs, rehabilitation, improvement and construction of infrastructure assets should be capitalized or recognized as PPE in accordance with IPSAS No. 17.

We recommended to stop granting cash advances to non-organic personnel in compliance with COA-DBM-DILG-GCGOCC-DND Joint Circular 2015-01 to avoid loss or misapplication of funds, otherwise the same shall be suspended/disallowed in audit in succeeding transactions

We recommended to the City Mayor to direct the City Accounting Office to create and maintain the said Special Account in the General Fund in order to easily monitor and track the utilization of the BGCM in compliance with DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.  Henceforth, comply with the requirements of laws, rules and regulations to support the National Government’s effort towards a unified, cohesive and orderly implementation of the national policy to address COVID-19.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
Require each department to submit a list of donations received, as well as the inventory of items not fully consumed or distributed, to the CDRRMO for their consideration in the volume of items to be procured, and for consolidation in their reports.
Require the CDRRMO to consolidate the records of the donations received, and prepare the complete Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, using the prescribed format. Submit the same to the NDRRMC and copy furnish the audit team.
For the succeeding relief goods distribution, prepare the list of recipients with complete information as to the purpose of relief goods distribution, names/address/contact of recipients, and description and quantity of items distributed.

We recommended to the City Mayor to direct:
The conduct of in-house training and/or familiarity sessions vis-à-vis COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977, and other related government accountability issuances;
The City General Services Officer and the City Accountant to compel the drivers to completely and properly fill-up the Driver’s Trip Ticket and to only submit the Monthly Report Of Official Travels every end of the month; and
The City Budget Officer to charge fuel expenses to its appropriate budget.

We recommended  the  following measures:
1.  	Henceforth, for the CSWD Officer to exhaustively and properly review the completeness and correctness of data in the SAC Encoding Template or system to ensure that the generated lists of beneficiaries are one and the same person named in the SAC encoding template and payroll;
2.  	Update the SAC encoding template so as to reflect only  qualified beneficiaries in  the system based on the accomplished SAC; and
3.   	Make use of new technology in the preparation of payrolls to prevent double claims or payment of grants/benefits to the same person resulting in the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended for the City Mayor, to direct:
The submission of a justification why the mentioned payments of overtime pay will not be disallowed in audit.
All concerned to henceforth strictly adhere to the above-mentioned Joint Circulars as regards the payment of overtime services.

For future donations-in-kind, we recommended to the City Mayor to designate a focal person to take charge of receiving donated goods as well as coordinate with the Accountant for the proper recording of donations-in-kind in the books.
We also recommended to the City Mayor to direct the focal person to coordinate with the CSWD and CDRRMC offices in the selection of beneficiaries for the immediate distribution of donations-in-kind especially of perishable items with expiry dates.
For proper coordination, the responsibilities of concerned offices be clearly defined and flow of transactions be presented in a flow chart for the benefit of all especially during crisis to avoid confusion.
Also, distribute the remaining unexpired food supplies in the stock room to prevent further wastage of the same and properly dispose of expired items.  Proper documentation and accountability  be observed at all times.

We recommended to the City Mayor the following:
Assess and evaluate the necessity of hiring of JOs and technical advisers to avoid wastage of funds.
Minimize hiring of JOs and technical advisers when duties and functions could be done by a few.
Make use of the available personnel in the department to avoid duplication of duties and functions.
For the technical advisers, we recommended to further evaluate the performance and qualifications of technical advisers whose work experiences were not aligned to the assigned field. Discontinue the contract of service whose duties are readily available and did not require special skills, their services can be dispensed with to save government funds and resources.
Discontinue hiring individuals who are beyond the retirement age to avoid audit disallowances.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
For the next batch of scholars for SY 2021-2022, require the personnel in-charge of the scholarship program to review and re-assess the current screening and evaluation process, and develop and utilize more strategic assessment tools that will objectively determine qualified applicants, and are capable of maintaining proper and complete documentations thereof;
Implement an independent verification process to verify the applicants’ claim as to their indigency or being in the poverty threshold. It may be possible to utilize the forty-six (46) job order personnel charged against the scholarship program to perform the verification; and
Prioritize the poor but deserving, and underprivileged students who wanted to pursue college, and consider discontinuance of the scholarship grants for post-graduate students, except for special cases and circumstances.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to enjoin the following City officials to do the following courses of actions pursuant to COA Circular 2015-008, to wit:
A.	City Accountant

1.	To transfer in the books of accounts the road networks carried in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. If not possible, coordinate with the City Engineer for the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

2.	Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: a) road lot, b) road pavement, c) drainage and slope protection structures, and d) other miscellaneous structures.

3.	Reconcile the subsidiary ledger of the road and its components with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure completeness.

B. 	 City Engineer

1.	Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads with identified cost;

2.	Determine the components of local roads identified in the inventory without corresponding cost and recognize the cost of each component at its fair value;

3.	Provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

C. 	 City General Services Officer

1.	In coordination with the City Planning and Development Officer maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map;

2.	Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. 

D. 	Inventory Committee

1.	Conduct physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network system.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (CDRRMO) to utilize the National Disaster Preparedness Plan (NDPP) as one of the reference documents in its effort of ensuring that the City and its stakeholders are able to better prepare to anticipate, cope with and recover from any disasters.

observations.managementAction: AAR 2019

observations.recommendation:

The CDRRMC should ensure that programs, projects, and activities for the disaster risk reduction and management be properly identified and be presented in such details in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP), and eventually incorporated in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (LDRRMP) and integrated into the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) which is an integral part of the Annual Budget of the City; and

2.	The Local Chief Executive to ensure that programs, projects and activities in the appropriation for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund in the Annual Budget are properly presented as to components (70% Pre-disaster, 30% QRF), and the nature of expenditures (PS, MOOE, CO).

The CDRRMO to assign at least one (1) personnel that will be primarily responsible to prepare all documentary requirements and reports, including the report on the utilization of LDRRMF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

1.	To unload the management of monitoring and reporting the above-mentioned properties, secure from the Sangguniang Panlungsod authority of the City Mayor to formally transfer the above properties to the respective users/beneficiaries thru a Deed of Donation or a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and

observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the CDRRMO to identify PPAs under LDRRMF that is aligned with its CILDRRMP and implement appropriate PPAs, especially to the areas identified in the CILDRRMP, which are vulnerable to disasters.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | As of the year 2019, there were PPAs already designed to promote mitigation of hazards that uninspected calamities may bring. However, not all areas indicated at CILDRRMP which are vulnerable to disasters were taken much emphasis on the said PPAs due to time constraint of the previous administration. Moreover, the new administration was still in recognition of the implementation of the designed PPAs by the previous administration.
observations.reason: Due to time constraint of the previous administration and change of the new set of officials, designed PPAs for this budget was not taken much emphasis not until the recent incident that took place in the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the CDRRMO to formulate and implement a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with the national, regional and provincial framework, and policies on disaster risk reduction in close coordination with the local development councils. It should encompass the four (4) thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | As of the year 2019, there were PPAs undertaken to generally formulate and implement a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with the national, regional and provincial framework, and policies on disaster risk reduction in close coordination with the local development councils.  However, not all PPAs under the disaster preparedness and mitigation was fully materialized due to time constraint of the previous administration and presence of new set of officials who are still in familiarity with the existing PPAs designed.
observations.reason: Due to time constraint of the previous administration and change of the new set of officials, designed PPAs for this budget was not taken much emphasis not until the recent incident that took place in the City.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Mayor to direct the City Environmental and Natural Resources, and City Health Office to take the following courses of actions:

Strictly impose the proper wearing of protective equipment and paraphernalia of the collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid wastes; and

Provide the necessary training to the collectors and personnel to ensure that the solid wastes are handled properly in accordance with the guidelines pursuant to the Act. All personnel assigned in the garbage collection must have a Certificate of Training as proof that he undergone proper training or orientation.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018 | To strictly impose the proper wearing of protective equipment and paraphernalia of the collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid wastes, the CENRO department head issued a memorandum addressed to every personnel concerned. 

Also, the CENRO coordinated with the CHO regarding the provision of necessary training to the collectors and personnel to ensure that the solid wastes are handled properly in accordance with the guidelines pursuant to the Act, however, since the CHO was still preoccupied with other underlying responsibilities, an update for possible set-up of training was done continually by CENRO.
observations.reason: The set-up of training was still held-up momentarily due to other essential tasks needed to be prioritized by CHO. Moreover, changes of assigned personnel as garbage collectors did happen upon change of administration.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Mayor to direct the offices concerned to do the following courses of action:

1.	Introduce additional systems and processes to address existing weak points in the collection such as but not limited to:

a.	Adoption of a regular monitoring system and/or closer supervision by a responsible officer such as but not limited to the following tasks: 

•	Inspection and review team to check that corresponding cash tickets are issued by the designated collectors; and that collections were properly made, receipted, remitted and accounted for; 
•	Adopt systems and procedures such that the issuance of cash tickets are consistently made; the possibility of non-issuance, recycling or misuse is minimized if not eradicated;
•	Surprise cash counts should be frequently made by authorized examiners.

b.	Conduct periodic reshuffling of collectors to deter familiarity.

2.	Require the contractor to immediately rectify the defects, since the defects took place within the warranty period. If this is not possible, the agency may use the 10% retention money in accordance with the provision of R.A 9184.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The CTO in coordination with OCEEM installed CCTV cameras with the said area which is vulnerable to under-collection and/or pilferages. As well as conduct of periodic reshuffling of collectors to deter familiarity. Also, surprise cash counts should be frequently made by authorized examiners.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to require the HRMO to coordinate with the heads of the departments and offices to incorporate or attach in their job order contracts a well-defined job description stating therein the specific work to be assigned to each JO personnel to facilitate comparison with the actual accomplishment reports, thus ensuring the protection of government funds from unnecessary expenditures.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Upon receipt of the AOM, the Accounting Department strictly notified all offices concerned that job order (JO) personnel were required to incorporate or attach in their job order contracts a well-defined job description stating therein the specific work to be assigned to each JO personnel to facilitate comparison with the actual accomplishment reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the above-mentioned AOs to maintain individual cashbooks per fund for their cash advances and petty cash fund to establish their accountability at any point in time. The same shall be duly certified and updated regularly to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Upon receipt of the AOM, the CTO department head issued a memorandum notifying every AOs to maintain individual cashbooks per fund for their cash advances and petty cash fund to establish their accountability at any point in time. The same  were also duly certified and updated regularly to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the City Treasurer to change the existing procedure in such a way that the CRCD and CRAAF shall be duly certified by the liquidating officer and forwarded to the City Treasurer to sign in the verification and acknowledgement portion before it is submitted to the accounting office for recording. observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to enjoin the following City officials to do the following courses of actions pursuant to COA Circular 2015-008, to wit:
City Accountant
To transfer in the books of accounts the road networks carried in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. If not possible, coordinate with the City Engineer for the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: a) road lot, b) road pavement, c) drainage and slope protection structures, and d) other miscellaneous structures.
Reconcile the subsidiary ledger of the road and its components with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure completeness.
City Engineer
Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads with identified cost;
Determine the components of local roads identified in the inventory without corresponding cost and recognize the cost of each component at its fair value;
Provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
City General Services Officer
In coordination with the City Planning and Development Officer maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map;
Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.
Inventory Committee
Conduct physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network system.

We recommended the following courses of actions:
Conduct physical count of all PPEs annually and submit RPCPPE to this Office pursuant to Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs;
For the GSO and CAO to perform periodic reconciliation of property records; and
The Disposal Committee or GSO to conduct inspection and appraisal of the unserviceable properties and recommend for its immediate disposal using the appropriate mode of disposal.

We recommended to the management the following courses of actions:
City Engineering Office (CEO)
Exhaust all options to identify the details and status of the abovementioned infrastructure projects that were marked as “unidentified”. Furnish the accounting office the details retrieved to facilitate subsequent actions to be undertaken.
Furnish the accounting office the certificates of completion for the completed infrastructure projects that were undertaken by contract. For the completed infrastructure projects undertaken by administration, provide the accounting office the details such as date started, date completed and other important details that would be the basis for the reclassification of CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to its corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
For the ensuing infrastructure projects whether undertaken by contract or administration, provide the accounting office the details through written communication including, among others, the certificates of completion (for projects undertaken by contract), and list/details of infrastructure projects completed and undertaken by administration. With this, the accounting office will have the basis for the reclassification of CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
The City Accounting Office (CAO)
Request from the CEO the copies of certificates of completion for the completed infrastructure projects undertaken by contract.
Request from the CEO the details of the completed infrastructure projects that were marked as “unidentified”.
Upon availability of the documents and other details coming from the CEO, reclassify the completed CIP-Infrastructure Assets account to its corresponding infrastructure asset accounts.
For the completed non-infrastructure projects, prepare necessary adjusting entries to take out the accumulated costs of these projects from the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account and charged the same against the City’s government equity.

We recommended the City Accounting Office (CAO) to revert those payables that are outstanding for more than three years which are determined to be without actual claimants through the preparation of adjusting entries.
For accounts that are not subject to reversion, we recommended the CAO to conduct further evaluation and if possible, coordinate with the payee/claimant and inform them of the amount due to them from the City of Digos. In addition, ensure that such accounts are supported with complete supporting documents.

We recommended the City Accountant to immediately notify the Bank in writing of the reconciling items under General Fund Proper – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-080,  General Fund Proper – LBP Current Account No. 0332-1029-58, OCEEM – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-030, SEF – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-032, and Trust Fund – DBP Current Account No. 0920-029240-031,  in coordination with the City Treasurer,  to adjust or correct the errors for an accurate and reliable cash account balance every month-end.

We recommended management to:
Direct the City General Services Office  to conduct a physical count of the breeding stocks and coordinate with the Accounting Office to facilitate reconciliation and/or adjustments in the books of accounts; and
Require the focal persons responsible for the breeding stock dispersal of the City Agriculture/Veterinary Office and barangay for the timely submission of relief from property accountability and the required documents on the disposal/death of breeding stocks as required under Sections 151 and 152 of the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Manual in the Local Government for validation and derecognition of the accounts in the books.

We recommended the CAO/CTO to coordinate with the payees for check replacements. Otherwise, make proper adjustment in the books to recognize stale checks following this  adjusting entry:
Likewise, we also recommend to the City Treasurer to conduct a periodic review of outstanding checks to determine which checks are about to become stale in order that necessary corrections and/or adjustments will be made.

We recommended the management to comply with the documentary requirements and conditions cited in the above Circular before granting city aid/subsidy/financial assistance to component barangays, such as but not limited to the following:
MOA
Approved Program of Work (for infrastructure projects)
Approved Project Expenditures or Estimated Expenses indicating the project objective and expected output
Certification by the Accountant that previous financial assistance has been liquidated, post-audited and accounted in the books
Official Receipts
Authorization by Local Sanggunian for the Local Chief Executive to enter into a contract in compliance with COA Memorandum No. 2010-014 dated April 22, 2010
Furthermore, specify in the MOA the submission of liquidation reports together with the complete supporting documents. The City may also set a condition that no additional fund will be released when a certain barangay fails to submit the same.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (CDRRMO) to utilize the National Disaster Preparedness Plan (NDPP) as one of the reference documents in its effort of ensuring that the City and its stakeholders are able to better prepare to anticipate, cope with and recover from any disasters.
We further recommended that the CDRRMO ensure that the minimum standards on the structure, system and policies, plans, data requirements, trainings, equipage, and service delivery, as established in the NDPP, are fully complied with.

We recommended the following:
The CDRRMC should ensure that programs, projects, and activities for the disaster risk reduction and management be properly identified and be presented in such details in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP), and eventually incorporated in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (LDRRMP) and integrated into the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) which is an integral part of the Annual Budget of the City; and
The Local Chief Executive to ensure that programs, projects and activities in the appropriation for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund in the Annual Budget are properly presented as to components (70% Pre-disaster, 30% QRF), and the nature of expenditures (PS, MOOE, CO).

We recommended that the City Disaster and Risk Reduction Officer, in coordination with the Accounting Office, submit the Report on Utilization of LDRRMF for CY 2019. Henceforth, the following courses of action may be considered to achieve timely submission of the report:
The accounting office to provide the CDRRMO the necessary data, on a monthly basis and not on a quarterly basis.
The CDRRMO to assign at least one (1) personnel that will be primarily responsible to prepare all documentary requirements and reports, including the report on the utilization of LDRRMF.

We recommended to the management to:
Expedite the implementation of the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan and stop the use of controlled dumpsite pursuant to Section 37 of RA 9003; and
For whatever the outcomes of the feasibility study, fast track the rehabilitation/completion/construction of the sanitary landfill without sacrificing the scrutiny of the design and plan as required by RA 9003.

We recommended that the CSWMB be reconstituted to conform to the membership requirements stipulated under Republic Act 9003 in order to optimize the involvement of the City’s constituents in the effective implementation of the plans and programs embodied in their 10-Year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan.

We recommended to Management the following:
Stop the practice of charging against the SEF expenses which are not related to nor included in the list of prioritized activities and monitor the proper utilization of the fund to avoid disallowances in audit;
Maximize the utilization of the SEF to attain its objectives and consider allocating amounts to projects, programs and activities that could address the needs of every school; and
Submit justifications as to why those expenses were charged against the SEF.

We recommended to the Management, through the Local School Board, to ensure that programs, projects and activities for the Early Childhood Care and Development be incorporated in the SEF Budget in accordance with Section 7 (b) of RA 10410.

It was recommended that retention money amounting to ten percent of the amount due the contractor be deducted to cover the cost of uncorrected discovered defects and third party liabilities pursuant to Section 6 of the IRR of RA 9184.
Accounting personnel assigned in the processing of claims on infrastructure projects was also recommended to attend a seminar on RA 9184 to make her aware of the documentary requirements in the payment of infrastructure projects.

It was recommended that the BAC serve Notice to bidders with Lowest Calculated Bid and require them to submit the latest income and business tax returns filed and paid through the BIR Electronic Filing and Payment System (eFPS) within five (5) calendar days from receipt thereof pursuant to Section 34.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9481 and paragraph 28.2 Section II. -Instructions to Bidders.
It was also recommended that the Technical Working Group include the latest income and business tax returns filed and paid through the BIR Electronic Filing and Payments System among the documents to be validated and verified during the post-qualification process which will be properly indicated in the Post-Qualification Report.

It was recommended to include Drawings/Plans and Specifications as an integral part of the contract agreement being entered by the City Government and various contractors in accordance with Section 6 of RIRR of RA 9184 and the use of a standard form of contract agreement provided in the Philippine Bidding Documents.
Also, as an integral part of contract agreement, Drawings/Plans should also be part of documents to be submitted to the audit team in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the submission of government contracts to the Commission on Audit.

It was recommended that the BAC and City government require the BAC Secretariat to post the Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed in the PhilGEPS within there (3) and fifteen (15) calendar days from their issuance pursuant to Section 37 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

It was recommended that extra care and effort should be exercised in accomplishing pertinent provisions of Section V. SCC of bidding documents to prevent confusion due to contradictory provisions with Section 37 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended the City Mayor to:
Direct the BAC to devise effective measures in furnishing the Audit Team with copies of the contracts together with the complete supporting documents within the reglementary period to facilitate a timely review, evaluation and inspection of the goods, services and infrastructure projects entered into by the City.
Direct the City Engineer to submit a request for inspection of completed projects to carry out the technical evaluation process of subject infrastructure projects.

We recommended the following courses of actions:
Stop hiring consultants when there are regular employees capable of rendering services to be required.
In case there is really a need for consultants, engage only on experts in their respective fields and observe the rules and regulations cited in RA 9184, specifically the Manual of Procedures for the Procurement of Consulting Services.
Submit justification as to why the management hired the abovementioned consultants without adhering to the provisions of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the CPDO, in coordination with the GAD Focal Point, to spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database, manual or electronic, consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information;
Ensure the effective implementation of the database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender-responsive planning, programming, and policy formulation; and
Thoroughly evaluate the data gathered from the database to come up with gender-responsive and gender-sensitive plans and programs which are in accordance with the GAD mandate.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the City General Services Officer to conduct an inventory of all the vehicles owned by the City and implement the required markings of “For Official Use Only” in a prescribed size of three (3) inches with the name of the City under it.
Require the City General Services Officer to coordinate and follow-up with the car dealer for the issuance of official plate numbers which shall be the basis for the application of government plate number with the Land Transportation Office (LTO).

We recommended to stop the practice of transferring cash advances to safeguard government funds to loss and/or misuse and to avoid delay in liquidation. Moreover, management may increase the number of bonded Special Disbursing Officers to cater the needs of the agency in times of major activities/celebration of the LGU.
Likewise, strictly adhere to COA Circular 97-002 to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance in audit.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the City Treasury Office (CTO) and the City Economic Enterprise Department to coordinate with each other and come up with a better set-up on how the two offices can work effectively to collect market rentals and enforce all the provision of the Code on Economic Enterprise.
We further recommended that the CTO and City Economic Enterprise Department coordinate and exhaust all their efforts to collect the above listed delinquent market rentals, and imposed necessary sanctions, such as 25% penalties of the amount of delinquency, 10% interests of the amount of delinquency plus penalties, and revoke the lease contracts of those registered market lessees with severe violations with the Code on Economic Enterprise.

We recommended to the City Mayor to direct the CEO and/or CAO to fast track the:
Improvement of parking area
Installation of the electrical and water lines
Formulation of operational plan for approval by BFAR so that operations of the CFLC may commence
Rehabilitation of the damages of the building

We recommended to the City Mayor the following courses of actions:
Require the Committee to conduct an in-depth evaluation of the CSO/NGO/PO applicants for accreditation before granting loans. If also possible, check the technical/professional background the officers/board of directors/board members/employees.
Consider re-evaluation of the qualifications and financial stability of existing accredited CSOs/NGOs/POs and require those who failed in the evaluation to comply with the minimum qualifications before granting loans and/or other financial assistance.
Strictly follow the guidelines of COA Circular 2007-001 and the newly issued DILG MC 2019-72 dated May 22, 2019 (a revision of DILG MC 2016-97).

We recommended the management to:

Direct the engineering department to submit the necessary supporting documents of the above-mentioned completed projects to the accounting department; and

Direct the accounting department to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify said Construction in Progress accounts to their appropriate asset account if the necessary documents are already at hand. Provide the appropriate depreciation expenses for depreciable assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the CDRRMO to identify PPAs under LDRRMF that is aligned with its CILDRRMP and implement appropriate PPAs, especially to the areas identified in the CILDRRMP, which are vulnerable to disasters.

observations.reason: Due to time constraint of the previous administration and change of the new set of officials, designed PPAs for this budget was not taken much emphasis not until the recent incident that took place in the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the CDRRMO to formulate and implement a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with the national, regional and provincial framework, and policies on disaster risk reduction in close coordination with the local development councils. It should encompass the four (4) thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery.
observations.managementAction: As of the year 2019, there were PPAs undertaken to generally formulate and implement a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with the national, regional and provincial framework, and policies on disaster risk reduction in close coordination with the local development councils.  However, not all PPAs under the disaster preparedness and mitigation was fully materialized due to time constraint of the previous administration and presence of new set of officials who are still in familiarity with the existing PPAs designed.
observations.reason: Due to time constraint of the previous administration and change of the new set of officials, designed PPAs for this budget was not taken much emphasis not until the recent incident that took place in the City.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Mayor to direct the City Environmental and Natural Resources, and City Health Office to take the following courses of actions:

Strictly impose the proper wearing of protective equipment and paraphernalia of the collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid wastes; and

Provide the necessary training to the collectors and personnel to ensure that the solid wastes are handled properly in accordance with the guidelines pursuant to the Act. All personnel assigned in the garbage collection must have a Certificate of Training as proof that he undergone proper training or orientation.
observations.managementAction: To strictly impose the proper wearing of protective equipment and paraphernalia of the collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid wastes, the CENRO department head issued a memorandum addressed to every personnel concerned. Also, the CENRO coordinated with the CHO regarding the provision of necessary training to the collectors and personnel to ensure that the solid wastes are handled properly in accordance with the guidelines pursuant to the Act, however, since the CHO was still preoccupied with other underlying responsibilities, an update for possible set-up of training was done continually by CENRO.
observations.reason: The set-up of training was still held-up momentarily due to other essential tasks needed to be prioritized by CHO. Moreover, changes of assigned personnel as garbage collectors did happen upon change of administration.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Mayor to direct the offices concerned to do the following courses of action:

1. 	Introduce additional systems and processes to address existing weak points in the collection such as but not limited to:

a. 	Adoption of a regular monitoring system and/or closer supervision by a responsible officer such as but not limited to the following tasks: 

•	Inspection and review team to check that corresponding cash tickets are issued by the designated collectors; and that collections were properly made, receipted, remitted and accounted for; 
•	Adopt systems and procedures such that the issuance of cash tickets are consistently made; the possibility of non-issuance, recycling or misuse is minimized if not eradicated;
•	Surprise cash counts should be frequently made by authorized examiners.

b. 	Conduct periodic reshuffling of collectors to deter familiarity.

2. 	Require the contractor to immediately rectify the defects, since the defects took place within the warranty period. If this is not possible, the agency may use the 10% retention money in accordance with the provision of R.A 9184.
observations.managementAction: The CTO in coordination with OCEEM installed CCTV cameras with the said area which is vulnerable to under-collection and/or pilferages. As well as conduct of periodic reshuffling of collectors to deter familiarity. Also, surprise cash counts should be frequently made by authorized examiners.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to require the HRMO to coordinate with the heads of the departments and offices to incorporate or attach in their job order contracts a well-defined job description stating therein the specific work to be assigned to each JO personnel to facilitate comparison with the actual accomplishment reports, thus ensuring the protection of government funds from unnecessary expenditures.
observations.managementAction: Upon receipt of the AOM, the Accounting Department strictly notified all offices concerned that job order (JO) personnel were required to incorporate or attach in their job order contracts a well-defined job description stating therein the specific work to be assigned to each JO personnel to facilitate comparison with the actual accomplishment reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the above-mentioned AOs to maintain individual cashbooks per fund for their cash advances and petty cash fund to establish their accountability at any point in time. observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the City Treasurer to change the existing procedure in such a way that the CRCD and CRAAF shall be duly certified by the liquidating officer and forwarded to the City Treasurer to sign in the verification and acknowledgement portion before it is submitted to the accounting office for recording. Henceforth, cashbooks shall be duly certified by the City Treasurer.

Cashbooks were now duly certified by the City Treasurer.

Ensure that only the authorized priority expenditures identified under the SEF mandate are disbursed and prevent the incurrence of improperly charged expenditures; and

Limit the utilization of the SEF budget only to programs/projects/ activities specifically defined by the law in order to maximize the use of the SEF for its intended purpose.

Hasten the implementation of the projects undertaken by administration. One way to address the problem is to  make a listing of all the projects to be implemented as well as those still on-going and preferably by source (General Fund Proper, 20% development fund, Trust Funds, Calamity Fund, etc.). Basically, the listing can be done by collating all the PPMPs relating to infrastructure projects. Based on that listing, calendar all the schedules of implementation and make an analysis if the projects can be implemented without delay  with due consideration  of the existing manpower of the CEO and the capacity to undertake the projects; and

Maintain a central database of all infrastructure projects of the city so that its status can be easily monitored. It also suggested that in the format of the record, included is an information or caption that will automatically indicate whether delay had already been incurred and how many days, so that appropriate action can be taken immediately. This record will also facilitate the making of accurate and timely report of Quarterly Status of Implementation of Programs/Projects/ Activities.

1. Direct the engineering department to submit the  necessary  supporting

project and the constituents as well.

mentioned project. Likewise, we emphasized that, in undertaking any kind of
projects, it must be properly plan and properly  documented.  As  possible,
complete the project in accordance with the plan.

We recommended to the management the following  courses  of
actions:

   1. Repair and maintenance of City Street Lights must be supported by Pre-
      Inspection Report to establish the necessity of the  transactions  and
      Post Inspection Report to prove that delivered materials were actually
      installed  and  operational.  The  said  reports  must  indicate   the
      specific Account Number, Lamp Type, Pole Number  and  Location;


   2. Strictly follow the provision in the Annual Investment Plan  (AIP)  as
      to which   office may implement the  program.  Likewise,  designate  a
      property  officer  who   will   accept   the    deliveries   so   that
      responsibility can be easily established;

   3. Submit copy of Purchase Order to the Office of the  Auditor  within  5
      days from its issuance and notify the auditor of the time and date  of
      the scheduled deliveries as provided by existing regulations; and

   4. Inventory items should be recorded and accounted properly.

We  recommended  that  the  City  thru  its  City  Disaster  Risk  Reduction
Management Office (CDRRMO), in developing the  City’s  Local  Disaster  Risk
Reduction  Management  Plan  (LDRRMP),  it  should  consider  the  four  (4)
thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management  such  as  disaster
prevention and mitigation, disaster  preparedness,  disaster  response,  and
disaster rehabilitation and recovery. This LDRRMP will be the basis  in  the
allocation of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF).

We recommended to the Chief Executive to require the  CCDRRMO  to  regularly
submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the  LDRRMF  through
LDRRMC to the Office of the City Auditor as required  under  item  5.1.5  of
COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that  the  management  should  consider  the  following courses of actions:

   1. Review the City’s Solid Waste Management Plan for purposes of ensuring
      its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation
      to local and international developments in the field  of  solid  waste
      management;


   2. Establish a system about the City’s waste diversion target.  The  said
      system should be congruent to the City’s Solid Waste  Management  Plan
      approved by the Commission. The  system  must  include  a  performance
      indicator to monitor the  status  of  diversion  waste  implementation
      particularly its goal  on the year 2023 that Digos City  should have a
      100% diversion  of waste generated from its disposal facilities;

   3. Monitor the implementation of the  City Solid  Waste  Management  Plan
      through its various political subdivisions and in cooperation with the
      private sector and the NGOs;


   4. Adopt measures to promote  and  ensure  the  viability  and  effective
      implementation of solid waste management  programs  in  its  component
      barangays especially the proper segregation of waste and establishment
      of Material Recovery Facility; and


   5. Intensify  the  information,  education,  communication  and  advocacy
      campaign  programs  of  the  City  especially  on   the   segregation,
      reduction, re-use and recycling of wastes to  encourage  constituents’
      awareness on Solid Waste Management Law (Ordinance).

We recommended to  the  City  Officials  and  Local  School
Board to:

   1. Ensure that only the authorized priority expenditures identified under
      the SEF mandate are disbursed and prevent the incurrence of improperly
      charged expenditures; and


   2.   Limit   the   utilization   of    the    SEF    budget    only    to
      programs/projects/activities specifically defined by the law in  order
      to maximize the use of the SEF for its intended purpose.

We required the BAC to thoroughly review the  processes  and  documentations
of all procurement transactions. Make sure that all documents  are  complete
and information therein be accurate and consistent.  Likewise,  observe  the
timelines provided by RA 9184 and its IRR.

We recommended that:

   1. The City should limit granting overtime services especially  those  in
      the performance of routinary  and  regular  work  and  activities  and
      properly observe the provisions of DBM Budget Circular No. 10 and  COA
      Circular No. 2012-003 in approving requests for overtime  services  of
      its employees; and

   2. The City should formulate a policy on rendering overtime  services  in
      order to ensure proper use of government funds for personal services.

Allocate 1% of IRA in its annual budget for City Council for the  Protection
of Children pursuant to R.A. No. 9344 to ensure that all plans for  children
are provided with funds and properly implemented.

We required the BAC to thoroughly review the  processes  and  documentations
of all procurement transactions. Make sure that all documents  are  complete
and information therein be accurate and consistent.  Likewise,  observe  the
timelines provided by RA 9184 and its IRR.

1. Review the BAC composition and provide complete information  of  every
      member particularly on their present position.   Likewise,  limit  the
      membership to government personnel involved in government  procurement
      as required under existing guidelines;

   2. Grant honoraria to chairs and members of the BAC and the TWG only  for
      successfully completed procurement projects or just consider once  the
      contract has been awarded to the winning bidder.  Also,  evaluate  the
      participation or presence  of  TWG in projects  attended,  since  they

We recommend to the management to adhere to the guidelines set by  the  law,
rules and regulations pertaining to procurement matters.

We recommended that:

   1. The management will establish a system on how to  collect  efficiently
      the loan granted to various NGOs/POs. Define the roles and  duties  of

      information that could help the management in evaluating  whether  the
      accounts are considered impaired or not.

We recommended that management strictly comply with COA  Circular  Nos.  96-
010  dated  August  15,  1996  and  2009-001  dated  February  12,  2009  to
facilitate the review and evaluation process.

1. Stop providing the JOs with overtime pay  since  they  are  not  among
      those authorized to render overtime services  with  pay  per  existing
      rules and regulations;


   2. Evaluate the work efficiency of every Job Order assigned  at  the  BAC
      Office.  Require them to maximize available working hours and practice

      and

Evaluation  System  as  required  under  Section  5.1  of  Joint  Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

Recommendations: We recommended to management the following courses of action:
Revisit the existing procurement process of the City. Identify in what stage where breakdown was observed and institute remedial action to solve the problem.
In addition, the City Agriculturist and the General Services Officer should exert efforts to produce/locate the documents for the procurement of organic fertilizers/vermicast production and procure the same. From there, proceed with the procurement of native chickens for raising and distribution to the beneficiaries;
Require the Office of the City Agriculturist to hasten the implementation of the project. Keep track of the whole activity from procurement, implementation and monitoring the status of the project.
Accordingly, in the identification of beneficiaries, the committee should consider not only the qualifications as a result of the screening process but also their willingness/commitment to implement the project. In this case, while the beneficiaries had attended the capacity building orientation and received the farm tools and implements in the first quarter of 2016, but to date some of them have yet to receive the materials they are supposed to plant.
Consider the distribution of these materials to the barangay level or directly to the beneficiaries. Further delay in its implementation would mean delay in providing the benefits to these beneficiaries. Even if the purpose of the project is noble but if not implemented, funds would only be wasted;
Ensure the timely preparation and submission of Quarterly and Terminal Reports to the NAPC; and
Submit the lacking documents for audit and evaluation.

Recommendations:  We recommended that:
1. 	The LCE require the personnel responsible in the implementation of these projects to fast track the preparation of the programs-of-work utilizing the funds provided for the purpose. If the funds are no longer needed, remit to the Bureau of the Treasury in order to make available funds for other LGUs;
2.	The City should maximize the use of the livelihood fund, and that any collection from the NGOs/POs should be programmed for release to other qualified NGOs/POs taking into account the guidelines of COA Circular No. 2007-01; and
3.	The City should remit to the Bureau of the Treasury the excess amount from the completed road construction projects totaling P51,240.59.

Recommendations:  We recommended the following courses of action:
Require the Local Development Council to come up with a detailed Annual Investment Plan (AIP) through consultation and deliberation with the concerned City and Barangay officials.

3.  Make sure that only those projects in the approved AIP will be implemented. Otherwise, the Sanggunian should pass a resolution for the reversion, if warranted.

Recommendations: We recommended to the City Government the following:
1.  Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by specifying in detail in the AIP the programs, projects and activities that are to be implemented by the City for the year.
2.  	Fast-track the implementation of projects which are already identified and evaluate the viability of projects which are not implemented during the year for appropriate reversion duly approved by the Sanggunian.

Recommendations:  We suggested to the management the following:
1.  Fast-track the implementation of projects which are already identified and evaluate the viability of projects which are not implemented during the year despite the availability of funds to determine the cause/s of its non-implementation.
Out of the 17 projects for implementation, 14 projects are to be implemented by administration and 3 projects by contract. In the implementation of projects by administration, consider the existing manpower of the City Engineering Office and the availability of equipment.
Funds are allocated on the basis of approved programs and projects. Thus, when specific projects are identified, it is expected that the need for the project must be met within the time duration for its completion.
2.	Evaluate the projects that are not implemented and cancelled/pending, resort to reversion if no longer needed.
3.	Require the Budget Officer to provide the complete detail of the projects to be implemented including its locations to facilitate matching of budget as against the projects implemented. Taking up of obligations for projects should only be based on the approved program of work.

Recommendations:  To address the aforementioned observations, we recommended to:
1.  Maintain a roster of participants on all trainings conducted and to be conducted to determine the actual need of each activity and make sure that only those concerned individuals are included.
Once the participants and/or recipients are determined, a ready typewritten/computerized Distribution List is suggested for a more accurate and readable listing.
We acknowledge with gratefulness the act of the Executive Officer in readily accepting his responsibility in the disposition of funds and immediately refunding the amount disallowed.
The management, in conducting trainings and/or seminars, should put in place proper controls to the different aspects of the events to avoid risks especially financial risks which may lead to suspensions, disallowances, or charges.

Recommendations:  To address the observations noted above, we recommended to give priority and/or fast track the realization of acquiring a loan from the DBP to finance the construction of the sanitary landfill as required under RA No. 9003 taking into consideration the immediate need to address the risks the community and environment are facing given the health hazards of an open dumpsite.
